text: This is Ahmed Khabib from New Cross Hospital. I'd like to share with you today this very interesting thirty-year-old gentleman who was running TEN-K but noticed diplopia when he used to look towards his side. He was diagnosed with acetylcholinesterase positive ocular myasthenia gravis and was referred for a radical thymectomy.

The surgeon begins the robotic thymectomy by inserting three ports on the left side of the chest: two 8mm ports and one 12mm port. An additional 8mm port may be used on the right side to confirm the position of the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon begins dissection medial to the left phrenic nerve, moving superiorly to expose the left innominate vein. The left thymic horn is pulled downward until reaching the inferior pole of the thyroid, where the vein is clipped off.

The surgeon mobilizes the left thymic lobe off the precordium, following the innominate vein towards the right thymic horn. The right thymic horn is drawn into the chest, clipped, and diathermy is applied to complete the dissection.
To note, as little as three grams of active thymic tissue can lead to recurrence, so it's very important to remove all the thymic tissue between both phrenics and from thyroid to diaphragm.

The surgeon begins dissection at the start of the superior vena cava (SVC), focusing on the tributaries of the thymic veins and arteries as they connect to the internal mammary vein and artery. The right pleura is opened to enhance exposure and facilitate retraction, highlighting areas prone to bleeding.
The surgeon mobilizes the thymus, focusing on releasing the most inferior part of the right thymic lobe from the area just above the diaphragm.
The surgeon moves the entire thymus en bloc into the left pleura and introduces a retrieval bag through the twelve millimeter port. The edge of the bag is visible at the port site.
The surgeon displays the innominate vein and superior vena cava (SVC), which are completely denuded of thymic tissue, extending from one phrenic nerve to the other.
Thank you very much. This was Ahmed Khadib from New Cross Hospital.
